title: Kashmiri Dum Aloo

overview.description: Baby potatoes fried until lightly crisp and then slowly cooked in a rich, aromatic Kashmiri yoghurt and spice gravy.

ingredients: 1 tsp salt, plus extra for the cooking water
method: Boil the potatoes in salted water until just tender but not soft.
Drain and allow them to cool enough to handle.
Peel the potatoes.
Prick the potatoes several times with a fork or skewer.
Heat the oil or ghee in a large heavy-based pan.
Fry the potatoes in batches until lightly golden on the outside.
Remove and set aside.
Whisk the yoghurt until completely smooth.
Add the bay leaves, black cardamom, green cardamom, cinnamon, cloves and cumin seeds to the pan.
Cook for 30–60 seconds until fragrant.
Add the asafoetida if using.
Reduce the heat to low.
Add the Kashmiri chilli powder, ground fennel and ground ginger.
Stir briefly, taking care not to burn the spices.
Add the yoghurt gradually, stirring continuously.
Cook gently until the yoghurt thickens and the oil begins to separate.
Add the water and salt.
Return the potatoes to the pan.
Bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover tightly and cook over low heat for 20–30 minutes.
Remove the lid and simmer briefly if the gravy needs thickening.
Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Dum cooking is a traditional technique in which food is cooked slowly in a sealed or tightly covered vessel, allowing steam and flavour to remain inside the dish.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to fry and then slowly braise potatoes, how to cook a yoghurt-based gravy and how gentle covered cooking allows a vegetable to absorb flavour.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic Kashmiri vegetarian dish that introduces the children to the dum cooking technique and demonstrates how potatoes can become the main event rather than simply an accompaniment.
chapman-tips: Use small potatoes of roughly similar size so they cook evenly.
Pricking the potatoes allows the sauce to penetrate more deeply.
Don't fry the potatoes until they are dark brown; lightly golden is enough.
Add the yoghurt gradually over low heat to reduce the risk of splitting.
The gravy should be rich and aromatic rather than aggressively hot.
Keep the heat low once the potatoes are added so the gravy does not catch.
Resting the finished dish allows the potatoes to absorb more of the sauce.
The dish often tastes even better the following day.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in airtight portions. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator and reheat gently. The potatoes may become slightly softer after freezing.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with rice or bread. The potatoes can also be lightly mashed into the gravy and used as a filling for a roti.
